analysis: Across the stronger heuristics the common and effective structure is: (1) deterministic Pareto prefilter, (2) cheap per-point proxies (axis-aligned box volume and small-weight-vector scalarizations) to rank and form a small adaptive shortlist, (3) greedy/CELF lazy-greedy selection using cached singleton (and when small, pairwise) HVs to avoid repeated exact calls, and (4) bounded 1-for-1 local swaps with conservative iteration caps and deterministic tie-breakers. The best variants balance shortlist size (quality vs. HV-call cost), reuse cached HV results, and apply vectorized score computations. Weaker variants either (a) do many full HV recomputations (GSI style), (b) use overly large shortlists or expensive per-candidate loops without CELF pruning, or (c) lack careful caching/upper bounds. Notable micro‑tradeoffs: more weight vectors improve HVC approximation but cost more precomputation (Heuristic 12); pairwise HV caches pay off only for small shortlists; Python-level set/list membership and repeated np.vstack/hv calls are hotspots. Deterministic seeds/tie-breakers are used consistently to ensure reproducibility. Overall the best heuristics minimize exact HV evaluations via tight proxies, lazy updates, and small targeted swaps.
experience: Design shortlists adaptively (scale with Pareto size and k), use cheap HVC proxies to prioritize, run CELF lazy-greedy with singleton+selective pairwise caches, and limit swaps to high-potential candidates. Add incremental HV updates or parallel HV evaluation, stronger upper bounds to prune, and avoid Python-side membership in hot loops. Keep determinism and measure HV-call budget versus shortlist growth.
current_reflection: Design shortlists adaptively (scale with Pareto size and k), use cheap HVC proxies to prioritize, run CELF lazy-greedy with singleton+selective pairwise caches, and limit swaps to high-potential candidates. Add incremental HV updates or parallel HV evaluation, stronger upper bounds to prune, and avoid Python-side membership in hot loops. Keep determinism and measure HV-call budget versus shortlist growth.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prefer small deterministic shortlists from cheap vectorized proxies, cache singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates to limit exact HV calls, and only verify promising candidates with exact HV. Bound swap iterations and shortlist size. Favor reproducible tie‑breakers and incremental HV updates; avoid full N^2 HV recomputations or dense pairwise HVs unless N is tiny.

Prefer CELF with cached singletons, adaptive shortlist size (function of Pareto size and k), and conservative pairwise caching only when shortlist ≲ 40. Use cheap upper bounds to prune exact hv calls, incremental hv updates for swaps, and parallelize independent hv evaluations. Tune swap budget and shortlist adaptively to maximize hypervolume per hv-call.

Favor CELF lazy-greedy with singleton (and small pairwise) caches, rank candidates via a hybrid box+weight HVC proxy, choose shortlist size adaptively (f(k, pareto_size)), vectorize Pareto filtering, cap swap checks, and validate only top candidates with exact HV. These choices cut HV calls, keep runtime predictable, and retain high hypervolume quality.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: adaptive shortlist, HVC proxy ranking, CELF lazy‑greedy, singleton/selective pairwise cache, incremental HV, seeded determinism.
- Advice: Scale shortlist with k and Pareto size, prefilter with cheap vectorized HVC proxies, run CELF with singleton+selective pairwise caches, batch/parallelize HV calls, cap swaps, and use incremental HV updates.
- Avoid: full N^2 HV recomputations, dense pairwise caching unless shortlist <= 40, Python-side membership checks in hot loops, nondeterministic tie‑breaking, and unbounded swap iterations.
- Explanation: These choices minimize exact HV evaluations, keep runtime predictable, preserve hypervolume quality per HV‑call, and ensure reproducible k‑point outputs.
